Paper title: Factors influencing wind turbine avoidance behaviour of a migrating soaring bird
Comments:
The paper study avoidance movements of black kites when facing wind turbines. It demonstrates that the movement of avoidance does not ocurr at not risky heights but it ocurrs when collision was likely to happen due to risky heights and trajectories.
Why move? seeking safety, i.e. to use a safe route since there is a turbine in the middle of the way (the more efficient route)
Where to move? towards a route that avoid the wind turbines, which would be a non-efficient route in regular conditions (without turbines)

This means that the risk of collisions between porpoises and the rotors of the tidal turbine in the current study is likely to be extremely low compared to if the animals were not responding to the turbine. " ... "Porpoises are generally moving below the rotor swept area, potentially to avoid the moving rotors. This has important implications for the design of future turbines or the placement of turbines relative to each other in arrays"
Why move? seeking safety
Where to move? Below the rotors
When to move? when turbines are rotating (this is less clear, but some evidences suggest it)

Paper title: Influence of Behavior on Bird Mortality in Wind Energy Developments
Comments: In this article, authors collected bird behavior data through "variable distance circular point observations" (observational data). The calculated an utilization rate (birds/session) and they classified flight behaviors (fly-through, gliding, soaring, etc.). Results show that Utilization rates (birds/session) were highly seasonal: whereas eolic power output peaked over summer, bird activity peaked over winter (WHEN TO MOVE?). Moreover, number of flights through turbine rows and flights within 50 m of turbines peaked during winter, when wind turbine operations were lowest (WHERE). In addition, the number of passess at < 50 m from turbines decreased with increasing proportion of turbines that operated during the observation session (WHERE). Lastly, for some species, operational status of the nearest turbine roughly corresponded with time spent flying to travel, forage, or interact with other birds while within the turbine rotor zone (HOW?). Tracking methodlogy: I indicated "Other" because in some cases they tracked individuals (time spent in each flight behaviour)

Paper title: Trying to choose the less bad route: Individual migratory behaviour of Atlantic salmon smolts (Salmo salar L.) approaching a bifurcation between a hydropower station and a navigation canal
Comments:
Not knowing much about swimming of fishes on rivers, the papers seem quite complex in terms of effects and results. It is a detailed description of salmons' movement when reaching a specific bifurcation during downstream migration. One option contains two hydropower stations, which in any case seems negative.
Spatial scale: large. The paper study a decision in the route of migration. The study is based on a local movement since it is about choosing one route or another at certain point of the migration but all is in the framework of migration, and they distinguish between continuing or stopping the migration. So, I am not sure about what spatial scale I should choose.
How? The paper indicates there is an extra cost of energy expenditure due to human infrastructures such as hydropower stations. It is not what the paper analyses, but it does indicate it based on bibliography.
Where? Fishes have to decide between to alternative routes of migration, one containing a hydropower stations and the other being an artificial navigation canal. 
Why? The paper indicates several explanations about why both options resulted in negative effects for the animals leading them to frequently stop migration, mainly changes on hydrodynamic characteristics due to anthropogenic causes that disoriented the salmons.
When? The salmons develop several attempts of crossing before deciding, so they can cross the first time or even the eighth time, deciding when to cross apparently based on the information they collect on each attempt or changes on hydrodynamic characteristics over time.

Paper title: Effects of hydroelectric turbine noise on the behaviour of Leporinus taeniatus (Characiformes: Anostomidae) in captivity
Comments: In this experimental study, authors investigated the noise impact of hydroelectric turbines on the behaviour of Leporinus taeniatus (individuals were filmed and variables were obtained for each specimen). Results showed that the fish swam significantly faster (HOW), moving to the bottom (WHERE) and swimming in depolarised shoals (HOW) during noise exposure in both groups. Temporal analysis showed that during noise exposure fish swam faster (HOW), were less stationary and used the bottom of the tanks more throughout sampling period (WHERE). These data provide evidence that fish behaviour can be affected by the aversive stimulus of the noise of a hydroelectric turbine in operation.

Paper title: Life history, latitudinal patterns, and status of the shortnose sturgeon, Acipenser brevirostrum
Comments: The manuscript is a review of the knowledge about the species. Throughout the manuscript, it is not always clear whether the authors are using references already cited regarding animal tracking (radiotelemetry) or not. In any case, among the references cited there are papers where authors used radiotelemetry, so I did fill the column "Tracking method" based on that. Moreover, the paper does not report a clear time window encompassed by the review
